problems.title: NumPy Version
problems.statementMd:
Implement `numpy_version()`.

Return the installed NumPy version as a string — the value available as
`np.__version__`. (For exploring, `np.show_config()` prints the full build
configuration, but this function only needs to return the version string.)
hints: NumPy exposes its version as np.__version__.

problems.title: Memory Size of an Array
problems.statementMd:
Implement `memory_size(a)`.

Return the total number of bytes the array `a` occupies in memory — the
number of elements times the number of bytes per element. Compute it from
`a.size` and `a.itemsize` (the `nbytes` shortcut is not allowed).
hints: An array holds a.size elements, each a.itemsize bytes — multiply them.

problems.title: Null Vector
problems.statementMd:
Implement `null_vector(n)`.

Return a 1D array of `n` zeros (dtype float). No `for`/`while` loops.
hints: np.zeros(n) builds a length-n vector of zeros.

problems.title: Null Vector With a One
problems.statementMd:
Implement `null_with_one(n, i)`.

Return a length-`n` vector of zeros (dtype float) whose value at index `i`
is `1`. For example, `null_with_one(10, 4)` is a size-10 null vector whose
fifth value (index 4) is 1. No `for`/`while` loops.
hints: Make a zero vector with np.zeros(n), then assign 1 to index i.

problems.title: Range Vector
problems.statementMd:
Implement `range_vector(lo, hi)`.

Return a 1D array of the consecutive integers from `lo` up to but not
including `hi` (`lo, lo+1, ..., hi-1`). For example, `range_vector(10, 50)`
ranges from 10 to 49. No `for`/`while` loops.
hints: np.arange(lo, hi) yields lo, lo+1, ..., hi-1.

problems.title: Reverse a Vector
problems.statementMd:
Implement `reverse_vector(x)`.

Given a 1D array `x`, return its elements in reverse order (last element
first). Use slicing — no `for`/`while` loops.
hints: A slice with a negative step walks the array backwards.

problems.title: Pairwise Euclidean Distances
problems.statementMd:
Implement `pairwise_distances(x, y)`.

Given `x` of shape `(n, d)` and `y` of shape `(m, d)`, return an array `D`
of shape `(n, m)` where `D[i, j]` is the Euclidean distance between `x[i]`
and `y[j]`.

The whole point of this task is to practice broadcasting, so you must
vectorize it.
hints: Think about 2D shape of the final result and how to get it from two input vectors (1D array).

problems.title: Closest Value
problems.statementMd:
Implement `closest_value(x, v)`.

Given a 1D array `x` and a scalar `v`, return the element of `x` closest to
`v` (the one minimizing `|x - v|`). No `for`/`while` loops.
hints: a.argmin() gives the index of the min element.

problems.title: Common Values
problems.statementMd:
Implement `intersect(a, b)`.

Given two 1D arrays `a` and `b`, return the sorted array of unique values
that appear in **both**. No `for`/`while` loops.
hints: np.intersect1d(a, b) returns the sorted, unique values common to both.

problems.title: Negate Values in a Range
problems.statementMd:
Implement `mask_negate(x, lo, hi)`.

Given a 1D array `x` and scalars `lo < hi`, return a **new** array equal to
`x` but with every element strictly between `lo` and `hi` negated (multiplied
by -1). Leave `x` unchanged. Use a boolean mask — no `for`/`while` loops.
hints: Build a boolean mask (x > lo) & (x < hi) and flip the sign of those entries in a copy.

problems.title: N Largest Values
problems.statementMd:
Implement `n_largest(x, n)`.

Given a 1D array `x` and an integer `n` (with `1 <= n <= len(x)`), return the
`n` largest values of `x`, **sorted in descending order**. No `for`/`while`
loops. (Hint: `np.argpartition` finds the top `n` without a full sort.)
hints: np.argpartition puts the n largest in front (unordered); take them, then sort descending.

problems.title: Indices of Non-Zero Elements
problems.statementMd:
Implement `nonzero_indices(x)`.

Given a 1D array `x`, return a 1D array of the indices where `x` is non-zero,
in increasing order. No `for`/`while` loops.
hints: np.nonzero(x) returns the indices of the non-zero entries (take [0] for 1D).

problems.title: Rows That Are Not Constant
problems.statementMd:
Implement `rows_not_constant(X)`.

Given a 2D array `X`, return the sub-array of rows that are **not** constant
(rows whose values are not all equal), preserving their order. Use a boolean
row mask — no `for`/`while` loops.
hints: A row is constant iff its max equals its min — compare per-row max and min, then mask rows.

problems.title: Sort Rows by a Column
problems.statementMd:
Implement `sort_by_column(X, k)`.

Given a 2D array `X` and a column index `k`, return `X` with its **rows**
reordered so that column `k` is ascending. Use `argsort` + fancy indexing —
no `for`/`while` loops.
hints: argsort the chosen column to get a row order, then index the rows with it.

problems.title: Cauchy Matrix
problems.statementMd:
Implement `cauchy(x, y)`.

Given 1D arrays `x` (length n) and `y` (length m) with all `x[i] != y[j]`,
return the `(n, m)` Cauchy matrix `C` where `C[i, j] = 1 / (x[i] - y[j])`.
Use broadcasting — no `for`/`while` loops.
hints: np.subtract.outer(x, y) builds the (len(x), len(y)) difference matrix; take its reciprocal.

problems.title: Diagonal of a Matrix Product
problems.statementMd:
Implement `diag_of_product(A, B)`.

Given `A` of shape `(n, k)` and `B` of shape `(k, n)`, return the diagonal of
the product `A @ B` as a 1D array of length `n` — i.e. element `i` is
`A[i] . B[:, i]`. Do it **without** forming the full `A @ B`, and with no
`for`/`while` loops.
hints: Entry i of diag(A@B) is row i of A dotted with column i of B — np.einsum('ij,ji->i', A, B) does it without building the full product.

problems.title: Gram Matrix
problems.statementMd:
Implement `gram_matrix(X)`.

Given a 2D array `X` of shape `(m, n)` (m rows / samples), return the
`(m, m)` Gram matrix `G` where `G[i, j]` is the dot product of row `i` and
row `j`. No `for`/`while` loops.
hints: The Gram matrix of rows is X @ X.T.

problems.title: Normalize Rows to Unit Length
problems.statementMd:
Implement `normalize_rows(X)`.

Given a 2D array `X` of shape `(m, n)`, return an array of the same shape
where each **row** is scaled to unit L2 norm (`row / ||row||`). Assume no
row is all zeros. Vectorize with broadcasting — no `for`/`while` loops.
hints: np.linalg.norm(X, axis=1, keepdims=True) gives per-row L2 norms to divide by.

problems.title: Trace by Hand
problems.statementMd:
Implement `trace(X)`.

Return the trace of a square matrix `X` (the sum of its diagonal entries).
Compute it yourself — `np.trace` is **not allowed** — and no `for`/`while`
loops.
hints: np.diag pulls out the diagonal; sum it. (np.einsum('ii->', X) also works.)

problems.title: Block Sum
problems.statementMd:
Implement `block_sum(X, b)`.

Given a 2D array `X` of shape `(n, n)` where `n` is divisible by `b`, return
a `(n//b, n//b)` array whose entry `(r, c)` is the sum of the non-overlapping
`b x b` block at block-row `r`, block-column `c`. Use reshaping — no
`for`/`while` loops.
hints: Reshape (n, n) to (n//b, b, n//b, b) and sum axes 1 and 3.

problems.title: Checkerboard
problems.statementMd:
Implement `checkerboard(n)`.

Return an `(n, n)` integer array filled with a 0/1 checkerboard pattern where
the top-left cell `[0, 0]` is `0` (so cell `[i, j]` is `(i + j) % 2`). Use
slicing — no `for`/`while` loops.
hints: Start from zeros and set alternating slices to 1: Z[1::2, ::2] and Z[::2, 1::2].

problems.title: Interleave Zeros
problems.statementMd:
Implement `interleave_zeros(z, nz)`.

Given a 1D array `z` and a count `nz`, return a new 1D array with `nz` zeros
inserted between each pair of consecutive elements of `z`. The result has
length `len(z) + (len(z) - 1) * nz`. Example: `z = [1, 2, 3]`, `nz = 2`
→ `[1, 0, 0, 2, 0, 0, 3]`. No `for`/`while` loops.
hints: Allocate the spaced-out array of zeros, then assign z into every (nz+1)-th slot: out[::nz+1] = z.

problems.title: Zero Border Around a Matrix
problems.statementMd:
Implement `pad_border(X)`.

Given a 2D array `X` of shape `(m, n)`, return a `(m + 2, n + 2)` array that
is `X` surrounded by a one-cell border of zeros. Build it yourself with
slicing — `np.pad` is **not allowed** — and no `for`/`while` loops.
hints: Allocate a zeros array two larger in each dimension, then drop X into the middle with slicing.

problems.title: Inverse of bincount
problems.statementMd:
Implement `repeat_counts(counts)`.

Given a 1D non-negative integer array `counts`, return a 1D array in which
index `i` appears `counts[i]` times, in order. For example
`counts = [2, 0, 3]` → `[0, 0, 2, 2, 2]`. (This is the inverse of
`np.bincount`.) No `for`/`while` loops.
hints: np.repeat(np.arange(len(counts)), counts) repeats each index by its count.

problems.title: Subtract Each Row's Mean
problems.statementMd:
Implement `row_demean(X)`.

Given a 2D array `X` of shape `(m, n)`, return an array of the same shape
where the mean of each **row** has been subtracted from that row. Vectorize
it with broadcasting — no `for`/`while` loops.
hints: Reduce along axis=1 with keepdims=True so the per-row means broadcast back.

problems.title: Swap Two Rows
problems.statementMd:
Implement `swap_rows(X, i, j)`.

Given a 2D array `X` and two row indices `i`, `j`, return a **new** array
equal to `X` with rows `i` and `j` swapped (leave `X` unchanged). No
`for`/`while` loops.
hints: Fancy-index the two rows and assign them back in swapped order: out[[i, j]] = out[[j, i]].

problems.title: Integers to Binary Matrix
problems.statementMd:
Implement `to_binary(x, bits)`.

Given a 1D array `x` of non-negative integers, return a `(len(x), bits)` array
of 0/1 where row `i` is the binary representation of `x[i]`, **most
significant bit first**. No `for`/`while` loops.
hints: Build the bit masks 2**(bits-1) … 2**0, then test each value against them with broadcasting.

problems.title: Scatter-Add by Index
problems.statementMd:
Implement `accumulate_at(values, idx, n)`.

Given 1D arrays `values` and `idx` (same length, `idx` values in `[0, n)`),
return an array `out` of length `n` where `out[k]` is the sum of all
`values[j]` for which `idx[j] == k`. No `for`/`while` loops.
hints: np.bincount(idx, weights=values, minlength=n) sums values into bins by index.

problems.title: Most Frequent Value
problems.statementMd:
Implement `most_frequent(x)`.

Given a 1D array `x` of non-negative integers, return the value that occurs
most often. If several values tie, return the smallest of them. No
`for`/`while` loops.
hints: np.bincount counts occurrences of each value; argmax gives the most frequent.

problems.title: Moving Average
problems.statementMd:
Implement `moving_average(a, n)`.

Given a 1D array `a` and a window length `n`, return the array of
length-`n` moving averages. The output has length `len(a) - n + 1`, where
output `i` is the mean of `a[i : i + n]`. Vectorize it — no `for`/`while`
loops (hint: prefix sums).
hints: np.cumsum gives prefix sums; a window sum is the difference of two of them.

problems.title: One-Hot Encoding
problems.statementMd:
Implement `one_hot(labels, k)`.

Given a 1D integer array `labels` with values in `[0, k)`, return a
`(len(labels), k)` array of 0/1 where row `i` has a `1.0` in column
`labels[i]` and `0.0` elsewhere. Vectorize it — no `for`/`while` loops.
hints: Index the rows with np.arange and the columns with the labels in a single assignment.

problems.title: Sum Over the Last Two Axes
problems.statementMd:
Implement `sum_last_two(A)`.

Given an array `A` with at least 2 dimensions, return the array obtained by
summing over its **last two** axes (result shape `A.shape[:-2]`). No
`for`/`while` loops.
hints: Pass a tuple of axes to sum: A.sum(axis=(-2, -1)).

problems.title: Normalize an Array
problems.statementMd:
Implement `normalize(x)`.

Return `x` z-normalized over **all** its elements: subtract the overall mean
and divide by the overall standard deviation, i.e. `(x - mean) / std`.
Vectorize it — no `for`/`while` loops. Assume `std > 0`.
hints: Compute the mean and std over the whole array, then broadcast them back.

problems.title: Replace the Max with Zero
problems.statementMd:
Implement `replace_max(x)`.

Given a 1D array `x`, return a **new** array equal to `x` but with its
maximum element replaced by `0` (leave `x` itself unchanged). If the max
occurs more than once, replace the first occurrence. No `for`/`while` loops.
hints: argmax gives the index of the maximum; copy first so the input isn't mutated.

problems.title: Solve a Linear System
problems.statementMd:
Implement `solve_system(A, b)`.

Given a square, invertible matrix `A` of shape `(n, n)` and a vector `b` of
length `n`, return the solution `x` of the linear system `A @ x = b`. No
`for`/`while` loops.
hints: np.linalg.solve(A, b) returns the x satisfying A @ x == b (don't invert A explicitly).

problems.title: Unique Rows
problems.statementMd:
Implement `unique_rows(X)`.

Given a 2D array `X`, return its distinct rows as a 2D array, sorted in
ascending (lexicographic) order — i.e. the result of treating each row as a
unit. No `for`/`while` loops.
hints: np.unique(X, axis=0) returns the distinct rows (sorted).
